definition: The hero required for a Team-Up to become active. The anchor usually receives a stat bonus while enabling one or more allies to gain Team-Up effects.
coachNote: Check anchor requirements before building around a Team-Up. If the anchor swaps, the Team-Up plan disappears.
example: Doctor Strange can anchor Arcane Order for Scarlet Witch and Magik.

definition: A hero who benefits from an active Team-Up when the Anchor Hero is present, often gaining a new active or passive Team-Up ability.
coachNote: Secondary Heroes are the payoff pieces. Pick them when your team is already committed to the anchor.
example: Scarlet Witch and Magik are Secondary Heroes in Arcane Order.

definition: A Marvel Rivals mechanic where specific heroes combine powers to grant buffs, extra abilities, or passive effects.
coachNote: Treat Team-Ups as draft incentives, not automatic win conditions. The comp still needs frontline, damage, healing, and map control.
example: Planet X Pals lets Rocket Raccoon and Jeff the Land Shark ride on Groot.

definition: A close-range teamfight style built around durable heroes, sustain, and forcing the enemy to fight in your effective range.
coachNote: Brawl comps want short paths, cover, and committed pushes. Do not slowly walk through long poke sightlines.
example: Hulk, The Thing, Wolverine, and Rogue are examples of heroes that can support brawl plans.

definition: A narrow map area where defenders can concentrate damage and abilities into limited space.
coachNote: Do not trickle through a choke. Rotate, use cover, force cooldowns, or push together with defensive resources.
example: A doorway into an objective room can become a choke if the defending team controls every exit angle.

definition: Abilities that limit enemy movement, interrupt actions, prevent ability use, or otherwise disrupt control of a hero.
coachNote: Track crowd-control cooldowns before diving or using an ultimate that can be interrupted.
example: Stuns, immobilizes, knock downs, and charms are common crowd-control effects.

definition: A high-mobility attack where heroes collapse onto a single target, often a Strategist, then escape or reset before the enemy team can punish.
coachNote: Call the target before diving. One hero jumping alone is usually feeding; two or three jumping together is pressure.
example: Spider-Man, Black Panther, Magik, and Daredevil are examples of dive-friendly heroes.

definition: Attacking from the side or behind, usually bypassing the Vanguard line to pressure Strategists or long-range damage heroes.
coachNote: A flank is strongest when your frontline is already pulling attention. If nobody is looking at your team, they will all look at you.
example: A Duelist wrapping behind the fight to threaten supports is flanking.

definition: A hero who can stay airborne for extended periods or indefinitely, changing how teams must control vertical space.
coachNote: Against flyers, assign hitscan pressure or force objectives where vertical angles are less valuable.
example: Iron Man, Storm, Ultron, and Angela are examples of heroes with sustained flight.

definition: A ranged attack that reaches its target immediately when fired instead of traveling as a projectile.
coachNote: Hitscan pressure is valuable into flyers and fast flankers because there is no projectile travel time to lead.
example: The wiki lists examples such as Hela, Phoenix, and Ultron attacks.

definition: Helping an ally who is under attack, usually by damaging, blocking, healing, crowd-controlling, or bodyguarding the threat.
coachNote: Peel is not only a support job. Vanguards and Duelists should turn when the backline is being collapsed on.
example: Using a stun or shield to save Luna Snow from Spider-Man is peeling.

definition: Dealing damage from distance, ideally from outside the enemy team's effective range, to make engagement safer for the attacker.
coachNote: Poke wins by patience and angle control. It loses value when forced into cramped close-range fights.
example: Hela, Phoenix, Hawkeye, Iron Man, Black Widow, Moon Knight, and Squirrel Girl are listed as poke examples.

definition: A hero's ability to stay alive through health, self-healing, shields, damage reduction, or repeated support.
coachNote: High sustain lets a team hold space longer, but burst damage or anti-heal timing can still break it.
example: A Vanguard with high health and defensive cooldowns has strong sustain.

definition: A common player term used interchangeably with Vanguard.
coachNote: When someone asks for a tank, they usually mean a Vanguard who can start fights, protect space, or absorb pressure.
example: Hulk, Groot, and Doctor Strange are Vanguards.

definition: A common player term used interchangeably with Strategist.
coachNote: Support does not only mean healing. Strategists often bring tempo tools, control, damage boosts, and fight-saving ultimates.
example: Luna Snow, Mantis, and Rocket Raccoon are Strategists.

definition: Damage per second; commonly used by players as another word for Duelist.
coachNote: DPS picks still need a job: poke, dive, flank, brawl, anti-air, or objective pressure.
example: Hela, Spider-Man, and The Punisher are Duelists.

definition: The safer rear area of a team formation where Strategists and long-range heroes often play.
coachNote: Backline players should keep escape routes and line of sight to allies. Frontline players should notice when the backline is pressured.
example: A Luna Snow positioned behind Groot and Punisher is playing in the backline.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals role terminology

definition: The forward pressure line where Vanguards and close-range heroes contest space, block routes, and start engagements.
coachNote: Frontline is about space, not just standing first. Good frontline play creates safe angles for the rest of the team.
example: A Groot walling a doorway while allies rotate is controlling the frontline.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals role terminology

definition: A side angle that pressures the enemy from somewhere other than the main team position without fully committing to a deep flank.
coachNote: Use off-angles to split attention, then leave before the enemy can isolate you.
example: Hela taking a side balcony while her team pushes main is playing an off-angle.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals positioning terms

definition: An elevated position that improves sightlines, cover options, and pressure over enemies below.
coachNote: High ground is valuable until the objective or enemy rotation makes it irrelevant. Do not stay up there after the fight moves away.
example: A Punisher holding a balcony above a choke can pressure enemies before they reach point.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals map-control terms

definition: A map location that gives a player or team multiple options to pressure objective, control lanes, scout enemy movement, and leave safely.
coachNote: Do not treat a power position as a fixed camping spot. Use it to create passive pressure, force enemy cooldowns, then rotate or drop when the fight asks for it.
example: A central high-ground room that sees objective and connects to both side lanes can be a power position.

definition: The ability to influence which routes, sightlines, and side paths the enemy can safely use during a fight.
coachNote: Think in main lane and side lanes. Strong teams do not only walk the shortest route; they open side pressure and make enemies answer multiple directions.
example: Taking a power position that sees main and a side doorway gives your team lane control without standing on point.

definition: Value created by a strong position before committing abilities, because the enemy must respect your angle, movement options, or threat.
coachNote: Passive pressure is not passive play. You are playing aggressively with position while staying patient with cooldowns and cover.
example: Rocket Raccoon holding an off-angle can force Luna Snow to panic-use a cooldown before Rocket fully commits.

definition: Using cover and distance from a cover edge so the enemy can see and shoot as little of your character model as possible.
coachNote: Being near cover is not enough. If your full body is visible, you are giving the enemy too many chances to hit you.
example: Backing up behind horizontal cover until only your head is visible is hitbox minimization.

definition: Adjusting your angle so the enemy is more exposed from your perspective while you still keep your own cover.
coachNote: Small left-right position changes can turn a nearly hidden enemy into a hittable target without making you wide open.
example: Shifting slightly left around a corner so you can see more of an enemy trying to hide behind cover.

definition: A cover principle where you try to expose yourself to only the angle you are actively fighting instead of several threats at once.
coachNote: If you are shooting one target, it usually makes no sense to be visible to three other enemies. Reposition until cover blocks the extra angles.
example: Moving behind a wall so you can shoot the enemy on the right while the enemy on main cannot see you.

definition: Gathering information about enemy location, movement, and cooldowns before committing damage, mobility, or a full engage.
coachNote: Scout from safety before you peek or dive. Good scouting makes mechanics easier because you are not surprised by the target's movement.
example: A Black Widow checking where Spider-Man is moving before charging and peeking for the shot.

definition: A peek where you move sideways along the cover edge instead of walking forward into the enemy sightline.
coachNote: Parallel peeking makes you harder to hit and gives you a cleaner return path back behind cover.
example: Peeking left from a wall, firing, then strafing back behind the same wall instead of walking toward the enemy.

definition: An exchange where one team's position or pressure forces enemy abilities, creating a window to punish with your own cooldowns afterward.
coachNote: Power positions are strong because they can make enemies spend resources first. Survive the response, then punish while their tools are missing.
example: A Strategist angle forces enemy mobility and a defensive cooldown, then your Vanguard engages while those tools are down.

definition: Map geometry or objects that block line of sight, reduce visible hitbox, and create a safe reset point during pressure trades.
coachNote: Cover is your default state, not a panic button. Peek with a purpose, then return before the enemy gets repeated shots.
example: A wall, payload corner, high-ground ledge, doorway, or box can all function as cover depending on the angle.

definition: A line of vision between two points that determines who can see, shoot, heal, or threaten whom.
coachNote: Control sightlines deliberately. A strong sightline pressures enemies; a careless one exposes you to damage you are not ready to answer.
example: Holding a balcony that sees the choke creates a sightline enemies must respect before they can reach objective.

definition: Briefly exposing yourself from cover to gather information, apply pressure, or take a shot before returning to safety.
coachNote: Peek with a purpose. Wide peeking or walking forward into the enemy sightline makes your movement easy to punish.
example: A player shoulder-peeks from a doorway, fires one shot, then returns behind the wall.

definition: The moment a team or player commits into a fight with pressure, mobility, cooldowns, or an ultimate.
coachNote: A good engage has a target, a timing trigger, and an exit or follow-up plan. Going in alone is usually not an engage; it is a feed.
example: Thor jumping the backline as his Duelists open side angles is an engage.

definition: The decision of which enemy should be pressured first based on isolation, cooldowns, health, position, and fight win condition.
coachNote: The best target is not always the closest target. Look for enemies who are exposed, low-resource, or already forced to look away.
example: Punishing a Strategist who used mobility early can be better than shooting a full-health Vanguard.

definition: A plan for emphasizing one part of a hero's kit through team composition, playstyle, cooldown priority, positioning, and fight timing.
coachNote: Marvel Rivals does not use item builds like a MOBA. A build is usually a coaching plan: what ability gets protected, what cooldown starts fights, what stat matters, and what teammates make that plan stronger.
example: Building Rocket Raccoon around revive, armor packs, and lane support is different from building him around damage uptime.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan that prioritizes a hero's non-damage fight tools: crowd control, revive, shields, walls, cleanse-like saves, scouting, disruption, damage boosts, or cooldown denial.
coachNote: A utility build asks, 'What tool wins the fight if I keep it for the right moment?' You play around timing, line of sight, and cooldown discipline instead of spending every ability for raw damage.
example: An Adam Warlock utility build protects Karmic Revival and Soul Bond timing so the team can survive burst and re-engage.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan centered on maximizing damage uptime, burst windows, target pressure, and finishing power.
coachNote: Damage builds still need setup. The mistake is treating damage as constant spam instead of pairing it with angles, forced cooldowns, and target priority.
example: A Hela damage build plays for clean sightlines, off-angles, and burst timing after enemy defensive tools are spent.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan that emphasizes staying power through healing, shields, armor, damage reduction, revive value, or repeated defensive resources.
coachNote: Sustain builds are strongest when they help your team hold or retake space. They lose value if the team only survives without creating pressure.
example: A Groot sustain build focuses on walls, body blocking, and cover cycling so allies can stabilize and keep fighting.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan centered on movement tools, rotations, chase routes, escape paths, vertical access, and fast target swaps.
coachNote: Mobility builds are not just about moving more. They are about arriving first, leaving safely, and forcing enemies to turn at the wrong time.
example: A Spider-Man mobility build uses web movement to scout, isolate a backline target, and reset before peel arrives.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A build plan focused on charging, protecting, and converting a hero's ultimate into a fight-winning timing window.
coachNote: Ultimate builds should not hold the whole team hostage. Use regular cooldowns to force enemy answers first, then spend the ultimate when follow-up is ready.
example: A Luna Snow ultimate build plays safe before the enemy engage, then uses Fate of Both Worlds to swing the committed fight.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals build planning

definition: A ranked-ladder distortion where smurfs or alternate accounts temporarily add high-skill players into lower visible ranks, making those ranks feel harder or more populated with strong players than they really are.
coachNote: Do not read every hard lobby as proof you belong lower. Smurfs can make a rank feel uneven because the account rank and the player's real skill do not match.
example: A Diamond-level player on a fresh Gold account can inflate Gold by playing far above the lobby's expected skill while climbing through it.
sourceName: Coach definition for Marvel Rivals ranked discussion

definition: Good game. Usually said after a match ends.
coachNote: Good game.
example: gg team

definition: Good luck, have fun. A friendly phrase used at the start of a match.
coachNote: Good luck, have fun.
example: glhf everyone

definition: Laughing out loud. Used to show something is funny or unserious.
coachNote: Laughing out loud.
example: lol that was wild

definition: Laughing out loud, I guess. A casual phrase used to show mild amusement, uncertainty, awkward agreement, or reluctant acceptance.
coachNote: Laughing out loud, I guess.
example: lol ig that works

definition: Be right back. Used when someone steps away briefly.
coachNote: Be right back.
example: brb one sec

definition: Away from keyboard. The player is not currently active.
coachNote: Away from keyboard.
example: our healer is afk

definition: Nice try. Used to encourage someone after a failed play or close round.
coachNote: Nice try.
example: nt, we almost had it

definition: Well played. A compliment for a good move, round, or match.
coachNote: Well played.
example: wp on point

definition: Short for difference. Often used to say one player or role outperformed the other side.
coachNote: A performance gap between players or roles.
example: tank diff

definition: Intentionally feeding, or playing so recklessly that it looks intentional.
coachNote: Playing recklessly or intentionally feeding.
example: stop inting mid

definition: Playing in a way that causes your team to lose, either intentionally or through very reckless decisions.
coachNote: Use this carefully. A bad play is not always throwing; look for repeated choices that ignore the team's win condition.
example: stop throwing and group before the next fight

definition: Frustrated enough that emotions are hurting decision-making.
coachNote: Frustrated and playing worse because of it.
example: I am tilted after that loss

definition: The currently favored strategies, characters, builds, or tactics in a game.
coachNote: What is currently considered strong or optimal.
example: that comp is meta

definition: A balance change that makes a character, weapon, ability, or strategy weaker.
coachNote: A change that makes something weaker.
example: they nerfed that hero

definition: A balance change or effect that makes a character, weapon, ability, or strategy stronger.
coachNote: A change or effect that makes something stronger.
example: the patch buffed tanks

definition: To ambush an enemy, usually with help from another player or from an unexpected angle.
coachNote: An ambush on an enemy player.
example: watch for the gank

definition: To protect a teammate by pushing away, controlling, or damaging enemies who are attacking them.
coachNote: Protecting a teammate from pressure.
example: peel for supports

definition: Short for ultimate ability, usually a powerful ability charged over time.
coachNote: Ultimate ability.
example: save ult for next fight

definition: Cooldown. The time before an ability or item can be used again.
coachNote: Cooldown.
example: heal is on cd

definition: Area of effect. Describes damage, healing, or control that affects an area instead of one target.
coachNote: Area of effect.
example: avoid the aoe

definition: Crowd control. Effects that limit enemy movement or actions, such as stuns, slows, or knockbacks.
coachNote: Crowd control.
example: save cc for their ult

term: #wood players need cas to get dope
definition: Likely a trash-talk phrase aimed at lower-skill players. In context, it probably means "low-rank players need casual matches, carry help, or easy lobbies to get good rewards or look good." The wording is not standard, so treat it as heard-in-game slang rather than a fixed gaming term.
coachNote: Likely trash talk about lower-skill players needing easy help to get good results.
example: #wood players need cas to get dope

definition: A likely insult for very low-rank or low-skill players. It is similar to calling someone "bronze" or "low elo," but with "wood" implying a rank below normal competitive tiers.
coachNote: Trash talk for very low-rank or low-skill players.
example: wood players need help

definition: Usually short for casual or casuals, depending on context. In game chat it can mean casual matches, casual players, or non-ranked play.
coachNote: Usually casual or casuals.
example: play cas first

definition: Slang for something good, cool, impressive, or desirable. In gaming chat it may describe rewards, cosmetics, plays, or gear.
coachNote: Cool, good, impressive, or desirable.
example: that skin is dope

definition: A position or line of attack that changes who can see, shoot, heal, or threaten whom. In Marvel Rivals coaching, an angle is usually about creating pressure from a useful direction while still having cover or an escape path.
coachNote: A position or line of attack that creates pressure from a useful direction.
example: Taking the balcony angle lets Hela pressure the enemy backline while her team walks main.
sourceName: Coach definition informed by Marvel Rivals positioning terms
